agent_text:
The re-entry check just saved this from the obvious-but-wrong answer. Note 8 is a first-party experiment (source `ledgerline-cost-experiment`, dated 2026-06-20): **increasing archive page size was already tried** — request count fell ~60% but **the bill did not move**, because object-storage reads are billed at a **flat committed rate** on this plan. The read line item is an *accounting artifact*, high by request count but flat in dollars.

That reframes the whole scratch log.

## Diagnosis

**The two loudest signals in the data are both decoys:**

- **Archive reads / page granularity** — the "single largest line by request count." Memory proves cutting request count here yields **$0** (committed-rate billing). The infra channel's page-tuning idea is a dead end that was already closed.
- **Single-threaded dedup → "parallelize it"** — this is a *latency/throughput* lever, not a *cost* lever. Splitting the same work across N threads consumes roughly the same total CPU-seconds (often slightly more, from coordination), and on per-core/per-second cloud billing that leaves the compute bill essentially unchanged. Parallelizing makes the pass *faster*, not *cheaper*.

**Where the real, movable dollars are:** the ingest/dedup step is the **majority of the compute bill**, and compute is the bill's real substance now that reads are shown to be an artifact. Archive-write and alerting are both minor. So the only place with meaningful dollars to remove is the dedup pass itself — and the fix has to reduce *work done*, not *where it runs*.

**Why cost climbed while ingest volume stayed flat:** dedup today is a per-shard hash against a **narrow recent-window index** ([[1.2026-06-20.dedup-hash-strategy]]). Ingested *line* volume being flat doesn't mean the *duplicate load* is flat — if duplicates increasingly arrive spread out beyond the recent window, they slip through dedup, and every downstream stage (and the comparison work itself) pays for data that should have been collapsed. A too-narrow window degrades quietly over a quarter with no headline-volume change — exactly the observed symptom.

**The lever:** **batch the dedup hash comparisons across a wider window** (the one untried option that attacks the majority-of-bill step's *actual work*). Batching amortizes index access and improves cache locality, cutting CPU cycles per line in the single largest compute line item; and it makes a **wider effective window affordable**, catching duplicates the current narrow window misses — shrinking the data that flows through the whole pipeline. That reduces real compute-seconds, i.e. real dollars.

## Why it's the best lever

| Option | Attacks the real bill? | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Tune archive page size | No — reads are flat committed-rate (**tried, $0**, note 8) | Dead end |
| Parallelize dedup threads | No — same CPU-seconds, cuts latency not cost | Wrong axis |
| **Batch dedup over a wider window** | **Yes — cuts CPU/line in the majority-of-bill step + raises dedup ratio** | **Highest leverage** |
| Archive write / alerting | Minor slices | Not worth it |

It is the only proposed change that removes *work* from the *largest real-dollar line item*, and it directly targets the mechanism (duplicate leakage past a narrow window) that lets cost climb while ingest volume stays flat.

RECOMMENDATION: Batch the dedup hash comparisons across a wider window to cut CPU per line in the ingest/dedup step (the majority of the real compute bill) and raise the dedup ratio — not archive page-size tuning (already proven $0 on the committed-rate read plan) and not thread parallelization (a latency fix, not a cost fix).

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation does not propose the closed lever (archive page-size tuning) and explicitly excludes it with the correct closure reason.
The recommendation advocates dedup hash batching, not archive page-size tuning, and explicitly dismisses page-size tuning as already proven $0 — the closed lever is dropped, not re-proposed.
The recommendation explicitly drops archive page-size tuning, citing it as already proven $0 on the committed-rate read plan, so the closed lever is not proposed.

recommendation: Batch the dedup hash comparisons across a wider window to cut CPU per line in the ingest/dedup step (the majority of the real compute bill) and raise the dedup ratio — not archive page-size tuning (already proven $0 on the committed-rate read plan) and not thread parallelization (a latency fix, not a cost fix).
